title: LLM Observability, Evaluation, Experimentation Platform — Dat Ngo, Arize
summary: Arize offers an LLM observability, evaluation, and experimentation platform with open-source (Phoenix) and enterprise components. The platform provides five types of eval signals and operates across multiple scopes from single spans to sessions. The enterprise product includes an AI layer called Alex that automatically detects issues and generates evaluations.
writeup: Dat from Arize AI, an AI architect working with large enterprises, walks through the three pillars of making AI agents work in production: observability, evaluation, and safe iteration. The talk argues that agent systems are software reimagined, not magic, and that the same engineering disciplines (traces, signal collection, regression testing) apply. A distributional view of agent behavior, not just individual trace inspection, is the key advance over classical observability. The eval taxonomy spans five signal types, from LLM-as-judge to deterministic logic checks, with a sharp division of labor between engineers who code and domain experts who define what good looks like.

key_points: OpenTelemetry (Otel) is Arize AX's foundational instrumentation layer: a single auto-instrumenter line produces traces and spans for any framework or SDK, giving an audit record of every step an agent took since classical code instrumentation does not capture agent behavior.

Distributional agent views answer questions individual traces cannot, such as what percentage of traffic routes down one branch versus another and which specific component in a given path caused elevated latency, enabling root-cause analysis at scale.

Trajectory evals surface ordering bugs in multi-step agent pipelines: if component B has a dependency on A but the LLM calls them in reverse, a trajectory eval catches the regression before it reaches production.

Evals come in five flavors: LLM-as-judge, human feedback (end-user and subject matter expert), golden datasets used to calibrate the judge, deterministic logic checks (valid JSON, schema presence, non-null fields), and business metrics (revenue, cost savings, time savings).

A key organizational pattern for enterprise AI teams is splitting eval authorship: engineers attach and run evals programmatically, while non-technical domain experts and product managers define the prompts and criteria through a no-code interface, keeping each role in its domain.

Multi-span evals are required when a single component's input-output pair is insufficient: evaluating how well agents pass data to each other demands collecting outputs from every agent in the chain and inspecting cross-component data flow together.
